did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper explicitly states that a machine learning algorithm was developed to differentiate various clinical forms of basal cell carcinoma (BCC) and benign neoplasms. It utilized features extracted from multimodal imaging (diffuse reflectance spectroscopy, optical coherence tomography, and high-frequency ultrasound) and employed CatBoost for classification, with feature importance analyzed using SHAP.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and validation of a multimodal diagnostic method using diffuse reflectance spectroscopy, optical coherence tomography, and high-frequency ultrasound on 37 neoplasms. It includes specific sections for Materials and Methods, Results with quantitative performance metrics (sensitivity, specificity, F1 score), and discussion of a machine learning algorithm (CatBoost) applied to new patient data, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Multimodal Method for Differentiating Various Clinical Forms of Basal Cell Carcinoma and Benign Neoplasms In Vivo
brief_summary: This study developed a multimodal diagnostic method to differentiate various clinical forms of basal cell carcinoma (BCC) and benign neoplasms using diffuse reflectance spectroscopy, optical coherence tomography, and high-frequency ultrasound. The method was evaluated on a dataset of 37 neoplasms from patients with Fitzpatrick skin types II, including five BCC subtypes and benign lesions. A machine learning algorithm was trained on extracted features such as melanin pigmentation coefficients, OCT texture parameters, and ultrasound tumor shape metrics. For differentiating specific BCC clinical forms, the model achieved a sensitivity of 70.6% and specificity of 95.9%. When classifying BCC versus benign neoplasms without regard to clinical form, the method demonstrated improved performance with a sensitivity of 89.1% and specificity of 95.1%.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study differentiated five clinical forms of BCC: infiltrative–ulcerative (n=2), pigmented (n=2), superficial (n=15), morpheaform (n=3), and nodular (n=5). Micronodular and infiltrative forms were mentioned in the introduction as clinical manifestations but not explicitly listed in the final 6-class experimental grouping. The study also performed binary classification of BCC versus benign neoplasms.
The study included 10 benign neoplasms (BN) which comprised nevi, fibromas, and dermatofibromas. These served as the comparator class to BCC in the classification tasks.
Morphologically confirmed diagnosis of skin melanoma was listed as an exclusion criterion for the study participants.
Morphologically confirmed diagnosis of squamous cell carcinoma of skin was listed as an exclusion criterion. It is also mentioned in the discussion regarding other studies using DRS.

all_task_types.target: Clinical forms of Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) and Benign Neoplasms (BN)
all_task_types.details: The paper describes a multi-class classification task to differentiate five clinical forms of BCC (infiltrative-ulcerative, pigmented, superficial, morpheaform, nodular) and benign neoplasms using a CatBoost classifier. It also performs a binary classification task to distinguish BCC from benign neoplasms without regard to clinical form. The model outputs probability vectors for class prediction.

datasets_used.name: Saratov State Medical University Clinical Cohort
datasets_used.description: A private, in-vivo clinical dataset collected from 37 volunteers (patients) with morphologically confirmed basal cell carcinoma (BCC) or benign neoplasms (BN). Participants were aged 40-84 years with Fitzpatrick skin type II. Data was collected using diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS), optical coherence tomography (OCT), and high-frequency ultrasound (US). The study was approved by the Ethics Committee of Saratov State Medical University (protocol №09 from 4 April 2023).

use.training: Used for training the CatBoost classifier via leave-one-out cross-validation (LOOCV), where all observations from one tumor were removed from the training set in each iteration.
use.testing: Used for testing the classifier via leave-one-out cross-validation (LOOCV), where the remaining feature vector of the excluded tumor was tested.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To train and evaluate the CatBoost classifier, ensuring that all observations from one tumor were removed from the training set in each iteration to avoid biasing the accuracy estimate due to potential correlations between data from the same tumor.
To assess the importance or contribution of features to the model prediction by quantifying the contribution of each feature to the final prediction for arbitrary machine learning models.
To assess the importance of a feature by its influence on the loss function used for training.
To reduce the dimension of the coefficient space by taking into account parameters correlating with each other, simplifying the classification model.

statistical_methods_used.method: Statistical texture analysis (First-order statistics and Haralick parameters)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To calculate statistical parameters from Optical Coherence Tomography (OCT) images, including mean, standard deviation, and Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) features for texture characterization.
To reduce class imbalance in the sample by assigning more weight to observations from smaller classes and less weight to observations from larger classes.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using sensitivity, specificity, precision, F1 score, and mean intersection over union (mIoU) with standard deviations derived from leave-one-out cross-validation, but no statistical significance testing between groups or against a null hypothesis was conducted.

Saratov State Medical University Clinica.experiment: Binary Classification (Differentiating BCC vs. Benign Neoplasms without regard to clinical form)
Saratov State Medical University Clinica.notes: For the BN class specifically, sensitivity was 84.0% and specificity was 95.6%. For the BCC class (without division into clinical forms), sensitivity was 95.4% and specificity was 94.6%.

architecture_used.input_size: 84 features (6 from DRS, 65 from OCT, 13 from US)

architecture_details.hyperparameters: Tuned through trial and error; specific values not reported
architecture_details.class_imbalance_handling: Weighted sampling (more weight to smaller classes, less to larger classes)
architecture_details.data_preprocessing: Scaling parameters to range 0-1; random assignment of parameter groups to observations to handle spatial inconsistency

classification_or_output_head.output_type: Vector of probabilities for each diagnosis
classification_or_output_head.task: Multi-class classification (BN and 5 BCC forms) and Binary classification (BCC vs BN)

preprocessing_techniques: Threshold filtering for OCT images to reduce noise outside the structural image (threshold = mean + standard deviation of pixel intensity in a rectangular area below the tissue)
Erosion filter with a window width of 3 pixels to reduce speckle noise in OCT B-scans
Two-dimensional smoothing filter with a Gaussian kernel (standard deviation of 1.5) applied to OCT B-scans
Scaling of all parameter groups to a range of 0 to 1 before model analysis
Random assignment of parameter groups from different modalities (DRS, OCT, US) to observations to handle spatial inconsistency
Weighted sampling to reduce class imbalance (assigning more weight to smaller classes and less to larger classes)
Principal Component Analysis (PCA) for dimensionality reduction of the coefficient space

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used to reduce class imbalance by assigning more weight to observations from smaller classes and less weight to observations from larger classes.

To handle spatial inconsistency of measurements from different modalities (DRS, OCT, US), parameter groups were randomly assigned to observations within the same tumor to avoid duplication and potential bias.

conclusions: The proposed multimodal method combining diffuse reflectance spectroscopy, optical coherence tomography, and high-frequency ultrasound with a CatBoost classifier can effectively differentiate various clinical forms of basal cell carcinoma (BCC) and benign neoplasms.

For the multi-class classification of five BCC clinical forms and benign neoplasms, the method achieved a sensitivity of 70.6 ± 17.3%, specificity of 95.9 ± 2.5%, precision of 72.6 ± 14.2%, F1 score of 71.5 ± 15.6%, and mean intersection over union of 57.6 ± 20.1%.

For the binary classification of BCC versus benign neoplasms without regard to clinical form, the method demonstrated improved performance with a sensitivity of 89.1 ± 8.0%, specificity of 95.1 ± 0.7%, F1 score of 89.3 ± 3.4%, and mean intersection over union of 82.6 ± 10.8%.

Feature importance analysis using SHAP and CatBoost built-in functions revealed that tumor shape characteristics (e.g., eccentricity, circularity) and melanin pigmentation coefficients were among the most significant predictors, with feature importance varying across different BCC subtypes.

The study acknowledges that the spatial inconsistency of measurements from different modalities (DRS, OCT, US) obtained from different areas of the tumor is a main drawback, potentially obscuring cross-modal correlations.

The small sample size (37 neoplasms) and class imbalance are limitations; the authors state that a larger, more balanced dataset and external validation on independent datasets are required to improve the algorithm's predictive ability and robustness.

Future work plans include integrating the three screening methods into a single measurement probe to achieve spatial consistency, optimizing preprocessing to handle imbalanced datasets, and potentially adapting the method for broader clinical categories or other skin diseases like melanoma.
important_remarks: The study is a pilot study with a very small sample size (37 neoplasms), which limits the statistical power and generalizability of the results.
There is significant class imbalance in the dataset (e.g., 15 superficial BCC vs. 2 infiltrative-ulcerative BCC), which may bias the model's performance estimates.

The method suffers from spatial inconsistency because measurements from DRS, OCT, and US were obtained from different areas of the tumor and randomly assigned, potentially obscuring cross-modal correlations.
The paper explicitly states that external validation on independent datasets is required and was not performed in this study.
Low sensitivity was observed for pigmented and infiltrative-ulcerative BCC, attributed to small sample sizes and the insensitivity of OCT/US to melanin or hard crusts.
No formal hypothesis testing or p-values were reported; performance metrics are presented with standard deviations from leave-one-out cross-validation.
The study population is limited to patients with Fitzpatrick skin type II, limiting applicability to other skin types.
Hyperparameters for the CatBoost model were tuned via trial and error, but specific values are not reported.
The authors acknowledge that the current approach does not correlate specific histopathological markers with measured parameters, limiting biological interpretability.
